titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 30 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender writes of a recently received letter, his recent promotion, and the spectacular effects of a new set of armaments the Germans have begun to use. The final page of the letter appears to have been lost.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. In July 1916, he was temporarily transferred to a Corps HQ on a separate section of the front. Throughout the war, Spender remained in contact with the Ulster Unionist leadership, and consistently opposed Home Rule and the six-county solution. This letter is part of a series of correspondence from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
30 July My pr I'm not sure if last night's
letter left in time for the mail
but as it was Saturday it won't
matter so much, as you will get
Friday's. Your letter of 24th has
only just come via the Army
but I ought to have got one later.
Rather surprised you don't say if
the family are pleased or whether they
think it makes you rather elderly
Mrs Temporary Lt. Col. However by the time
you are used to it you will be Mrs Captain
again! I don't know what the war office will
do with Reserve officers after the war but
think they will probably revert them to their
old ranks in order to prevent too many
senior officers from cumbering the land. You will consider whether it is advisable
to send Rex's letters to me or to Mater
or perhaps let Ev decide this. I saw a good deal of the battle
yesterday from near the ruins of an
old German village. There were explosions
of red smoke over another village
in front very wonderful cloud effects but
meaning I'm afraid some further new
German monstrosity. Later there were
some near shells bursting in the air
giving enormous cloud showers of
smoke and flame. None of us had
seen the like before. I have not yet
heard what their effect was. A great
fat budget so I must have another page
which you can pass round.
